This forced the operator to process data in extremely small chunks, leading to a massive increase in the number of loop iterations and associated kernel overhead. On Ascend hardware, memory transfers (DMA) and vector instructions (SIMD) are most efficient when operating on large, aligned blocks of data. A tile size of 64 elements (256 bytes for FP32) fails to saturate the memory bus and underutilizes the vector processor's pipeline. By removing this cap, the fast version allows the tiling logic to maximize the data processed per iteration based on actual hardware constraints, significantly improving memory throughput and reducing the total number of synchronization and instruction cycles.
bottleneck: Excessive loop overhead and inefficient memory bandwidth utilization caused by an artificially small tile size.

title: Increased Tiling Size to Improve Vectorization Efficiency and Reduce Loop Overhead
description: The optimization increases the maximum tile length cap from 16 to 64 elements. In the Ascend C architecture, the vector processor (AIV) typically operates on 256-byte blocks. For FP32 data (4 bytes per element), a tile size of 64 elements exactly matches this 256-byte vector width, allowing for full utilization of the SIMD instructions. The slow version's cap of 16 elements (64 bytes) resulted in only 25% hardware utilization per vector instruction and quadrupled the number of loop iterations. By increasing the tile size, the operator significantly reduces the cumulative overhead of loop control logic, address calculations, and DMA synchronization, while maximizing the throughput of the vector computation unit.
bottleneck: Excessive loop overhead and underutilization of the SIMD vector unit caused by an artificially small tile size (16 elements).

title: Elimination of redundant type casting and intermediate buffering for FP32
description: The optimization introduces compile-time type checking using `if constexpr`. In the slow version, the kernel always allocated intermediate FP32 buffers (`resultTmpBuf` and `calcValueLocal`) and executed `Cast` instructions to convert input data to FP32 before computation, and then back to the output type. For FP32 inputs, these operations are identity transforms that consume Vector Unit cycles and increase memory latency without changing the data. The fast version bypasses the casting logic and temporary buffer allocation when `inType` is `float`, performing the `Sub`, `Muls`, and `Mul` operations directly on the input tensors. This reduces the total instruction count, eliminates redundant data movement within the UB, and lowers the memory pressure, resulting in a performance gain of approximately 5.7%.
bottleneck: The original implementation forced an unnecessary casting path (Cast to FP32) even when the input data type was already FP32, leading to significant instruction overhead and wasted Unified Buffer (UB) space.

title: Elimination of redundant vector arithmetic instructions
description: The slow version contained a redundant `Adds` instruction that added `0.0f` to the result. In the Ascend C architecture, every vector instruction (such as Sub, Mul, Muls, and Adds) is dispatched to the Vector Engine and consumes a specific number of clock cycles proportional to the data length and the hardware's throughput. By removing this identity operation, the kernel eliminates an unnecessary pass through the vector pipeline. This directly reduces the total execution latency of the Compute stage per tile, decreases the pressure on the instruction dispatcher, and prevents unnecessary write-back operations to the Local Memory (VECCALC), thereby improving the overall throughput of the operator.
bottleneck: Unnecessary instruction overhead and pipeline stalls in the Vector Unit (VE) due to a no-op calculation.

title: Multi-core Parallelization and Hierarchical Vector Reduction
description:
The optimization addresses two critical performance limiters:
1. Parallelism: The original tiling logic forced the entire workload onto a single core (NUM_1), failing to utilize the multi-core architecture of the Ascend AI Processor. The fast version implements a dynamic tiling strategy that queries the hardware for the total number of available AIV cores and distributes the batch dimension across them, achieving a near-linear speedup proportional to the core count.
2. Vector Instruction Throughput: The reduction logic (GetReduceMax) was upgraded from a generic, small-scale reduction to a multi-stage hierarchical approach. For large feature lengths (e.g., 8192 elements), it utilizes AscendC::BlockReduceMax to perform partial reductions in parallel across vector blocks, followed by AscendC::WholeReduceMax. This strategy leverages the hardware's SIMD reduction trees and minimizes pipeline stalls compared to the original iterative "Small" reduction path, significantly reducing the cycles required for the Max operation in the Cross Entropy calculation.
bottleneck: The operator was bottlenecked by serial execution on a single AIV core and inefficient scalar-heavy reduction logic for large feature dimensions.

title: Vectorized Reduction for Exponential Summation
description: The slow version performs a manual summation of the exponential results using a standard C++ for-loop. This approach executes on the scalar unit, processing one element at a time, which fails to utilize the high-throughput SIMD (Single Instruction, Multiple Data) capabilities of the Ascend AI Core's Vector Unit. In contrast, the fast version replaces this scalar loop with a vectorized reduction strategy using the GetReduceSum method, which leverages the WholeReduceSum hardware intrinsic. This intrinsic utilizes the Vector Unit to perform parallel reductions across multiple data blocks simultaneously. By shifting the summation from the scalar pipeline to the vector pipeline and using hardware-accelerated reduction instructions, the operator significantly increases throughput and reduces the cycles required for batch summation, leading to the observed ~10x performance improvement.
bottleneck: Scalar accumulation of elements in the Unified Buffer (UB) creates a massive serial bottleneck on the AI Core's scalar unit.

title: Software Pipelining and Vectorized Instruction Chunking
description:
The optimization implements a three-stage software pipeline and restores vectorized data processing. In the slow version, the kernel processed only one row at a time (vecS1BaseSize = 1), which failed to saturate the SIMD vector units and resulted in high instruction overhead. The fast version calculates an optimal `vec1S1BaseSize` and `vec2S1BaseSize` based on the head dimension and available buffer space, enabling the hardware to process multiple rows per vector instruction. 

Additionally, the `Process()` loop was refactored into a pipeline using a task-rotation strategy (`extraInfo[3]`). By utilizing asynchronous execution and hardware synchronization events (`HardEvent::MTE3_MTE2`), the kernel overlaps the execution of the Matmul (Cube unit) for the current tile with the Vector processing (Softmax/Scaling) of the previous tile. This effectively hides the latency of vector operations and memory copies. The performance is further bolstered by re-enabling `SetFixSplit` in the host tiling, which optimizes the Matmul tiling strategy for head dimensions between 64 and 128, and by using block-based `DataCopy` instead of `DataCopyPad` when 32B alignment is met.
bottleneck: Sequential execution of Cube and Vector units combined with sub-optimal SIMD utilization due to single-row processing (vecS1BaseSize=1).

title: Multi-core Parallelism via AIV Core Scaling
description: The optimization replaces a hardcoded single-core execution constraint (aivNum = 1) with a dynamic query of the platform's actual AI Vector core count using `ascendcPlatform.GetCoreNumAiv()`. In the Ascend architecture, the `BlockDim` determines how many hardware cores are dispatched to execute the kernel. By scaling the number of cores from 1 to the hardware maximum (typically 30 or more on Ascend 910 series), the workload is partitioned across multiple cores (Data Parallelism). This allows the operator to process different data tiles simultaneously across the AIV SIMD units, drastically increasing throughput and reducing the total execution time from ~3.8ms to ~0.1ms.
bottleneck: Single-core execution bottleneck where the entire computational workload is serialized on one AI Vector (AIV) core, failing to utilize the hardware's parallel processing capabilities.

title: Multi-core Parallelism and UB-Aware Tiling Optimization
description: The optimization resolves two primary bottlenecks in the tiling logic. First, it enables multi-core parallelism by replacing the hardcoded single-core limit (aivNum = 1) with the actual hardware core count (GetCoreNumAiv()), allowing the workload to be distributed across all available AI Vector cores. Second, it replaces a degenerate tiling strategy (tileLength = 1) with a calculation that maximizes the tile size based on the available Unified Buffer (UB) capacity. By increasing the tile length, the operator significantly reduces the overhead of loop control and synchronization, improves memory bandwidth utilization through larger burst transfers (DMA), and allows the vector engine to better utilize SIMD instructions by processing larger contiguous data blocks per iteration.
bottleneck: The operator was constrained to single-core execution with a minimal tile size, resulting in massive underutilization of hardware resources and excessive instruction overhead.

title: Multi-core Parallelism and UB-aware Tiling Optimization
description:
The optimization significantly improves performance by addressing three critical areas: 
1. Multi-core Parallelism: The fast version queries the actual hardware core count (GetCoreNumAiv) instead of forcing single-core execution, allowing the workload to be distributed across all available AI Vector cores. 
2. Tiling Efficiency: The slow version forced a tile length of 1, which is the worst-case scenario for Ascend C operators. This caused the kernel to incur the full overhead of the pipeline (CopyIn, Compute, CopyOut) and synchronization (PipeBarrier) for every single element. The fast version calculates an optimal tile size based on the Unified Buffer (UB) capacity, maximizing data reuse and minimizing the frequency of DMA transfers and loop overhead. 
3. Instruction Reduction: Removing the redundant identity scaling (Muls by 1.0f) eliminates unnecessary vector compute cycles and associated pipeline barriers (PIPE_V), further streamlining the execution flow within the vector processor.
bottleneck: The operator was bottlenecked by single-core execution and extreme instruction/DMA overhead due to a degenerate tiling strategy (processing one element at a time).

title: Optimized Tiling Strategy for Unified Buffer Utilization
description: In the slow version, the tiling logic is artificially constrained by setting `tileLength` to 1. This forces the operator to process data in the smallest possible increments, leading to several performance bottlenecks: 1) High instruction overhead from the tiling loop; 2) Inefficient use of the DMA engine, as small data transfers cannot saturate the memory bandwidth; and 3) Poor utilization of the Vector Engine (SIMD), which is designed to operate on contiguous blocks of data (typically 256 bytes per cycle). The fast version implements a UB-aware tiling strategy that calculates the maximum feasible `tileLength` based on the actual Unified Buffer size and the memory footprint of the intermediate tensors. This allows for larger DMA bursts and enables the vector instructions to process significantly more elements per iteration, maximizing hardware throughput and reducing the total number of synchronization points.
bottleneck: The use of a minimal tile size (tileLength = 1) causes extreme overhead in loop control, memory synchronization, and inefficient DMA transfers.

title: UB-aware Tiling and Redundant Instruction Elimination
description: The optimization addresses two primary performance bottlenecks. First, it replaces a hardcoded, minimal tiling factor (tileLength = 1) with a dynamic calculation based on the actual Unified Buffer (UB) size. By maximizing the tile size to fit the available UB memory, the operator reduces the total number of loop iterations required to process the outer dimension. This significantly decreases the overhead associated with DMA transfer scheduling, synchronization barriers (PipeBarriers), and loop control logic, while improving DMA burst efficiency. Second, the removal of a redundant identity multiplication (Muls with a factor of 1.0f) and its subsequent PipeBarrier eliminates unnecessary cycles on the Vector Engine and prevents avoidable pipeline stalls. Together, these changes improve the instruction-level parallelism and ensure the hardware spends more time on meaningful computation rather than management overhead.
bottleneck: Excessive loop overhead and redundant vector operations that underutilize the hardware's compute and memory bandwidth.

title: Removal of redundant identity vector operation and synchronization barrier
description: The optimization removes a redundant `Muls` operation that performed an identity scaling (multiplying by 1.0f) and its associated `PipeBarrier<PIPE_V>()`. In the Ascend C architecture, every vector instruction issued to the Vector pipe consumes execution cycles and hardware resources, even if the operation does not change the data values. Furthermore, the `PipeBarrier<PIPE_V>` instruction forces the pipeline to wait for all previous vector operations to complete before proceeding, which can introduce pipeline bubbles and reduce instruction-level parallelism (ILP). Removing these unnecessary calls directly reduces the kernel's execution time by decreasing the total instruction count and improving pipeline throughput.
bottleneck: Unnecessary compute overhead and pipeline synchronization stalls caused by redundant instructions.

title: Enable Multi-Core Parallel Processing
description: The slow version explicitly forced the operator to run on a single core by setting `coreNum = 1`, which disabled parallel tiling and execution. In the fast version, this restriction is removed, allowing the tiling logic to use the actual number of available AI Cores (retrieved via `GetCoreNum()`). By passing the correct core count to `SetBlockDim`, the system dispatches the kernel across multiple hardware units simultaneously. This enables data-level parallelism where each core processes a distinct tile of the input tensor in parallel, significantly reducing the total execution time from ~3.7ms to ~0.75ms, leveraging the SIMD architecture of the Ascend AI Cores more effectively.
bottleneck: Single-core execution bottleneck where the entire workload is serialized on one AI Core, failing to utilize the hardware's parallel compute resources.

title: Double Buffering for Pipeline Parallelism
description: The optimization increases BUFFER_NUM from 1 to 2, which enables the double buffering mechanism in the Ascend C programming model. In the slow version (BUFFER_NUM = 1), the pipeline stages (CopyIn, ProcessCompute, and CopyOut) are executed sequentially; the Vector Engine must wait for the Memory Transfer Engine (MTE2) to finish loading data, and the MTE3 must wait for the Vector Engine to finish computation before writing back. By using two buffers, the MTE and Vector engines can operate in parallel: while the Vector Engine processes data in the first buffer, the MTE2 can prefetch the next tile into the second buffer, and the MTE3 can concurrently output the results of the previous tile. This effectively hides memory access latency and significantly improves the overall throughput of the operator by overlapping communication and computation.
bottleneck: Serial execution of memory transfers and vector computations leading to hardware underutilization.

title: Maximizing Unified Buffer (UB) Utilization and Reducing Loop Overhead
description: The slow version artificially constrained the tiling size (ubPartDataNum) to a single block (32 elements), regardless of the actual hardware capacity. This forced the kernel to process data in tiny increments, leading to a massive number of loop iterations and high instruction overhead for loop control and address arithmetic. Furthermore, small data transfers between Global Memory and the Unified Buffer (UB) are highly inefficient on the Ascend architecture, as the DMA (Data Move) engine performs significantly better with larger, contiguous bursts. By removing the hardcoded restriction, the fast version allows the tiling logic to calculate an optimal tile size based on the actual UB capacity (typically several kilobytes). This optimization maximizes memory throughput, reduces the total number of kernel synchronization points, and allows the Vector Engine to operate on larger SIMD vectors, resulting in the observed ~99x performance improvement.
bottleneck: Excessive loop control overhead and inefficient memory bandwidth utilization caused by an extremely small tiling size (32 elements per iteration).

title: Removal of redundant vector cast instructions
description: The optimization removes redundant Cast operations in the ComputeFp16 and ComputeFp functions. In the slow version, the code performed an additional round-trip cast (int8_t to half and back to int8_t) on the output tensor yLocal after the result had already been correctly computed and stored. Since these operations were idempotent and did not alter the data values, they served no functional purpose but consumed significant cycles on the Vector Engine. By eliminating these unnecessary instructions, the kernel reduces the instruction count per tile, decreases the pressure on the vector pipeline, and lowers the overall execution latency, which is particularly impactful in element-wise operators where performance is sensitive to the instruction-to-byte ratio.
bottleneck: Vector instruction pipeline overhead and unnecessary execution latency

title: Consolidation of fragmented DMA transfers to reduce instruction and startup overhead
description:
The optimization consolidates two split `DataCopy` operations into a single contiguous transfer for each input tensor. In the Ascend C architecture, `DataCopy` triggers the DMA engine (MTE2) to move data between Global Memory and the Unified Buffer. Each DMA transfer incurs a fixed startup latency and instruction overhead. 

The slow version artificially bisects the data into `firstLen` and `secondLen`, resulting in four DMA calls per loop iteration (two for each input tensor) and additional scalar logic/branching. The fast version reduces this to two DMA calls per iteration. Because the tiling strategy (defined in `op_host`) forces an extremely small tile size of 32 elements (`BLOCK_SIZE`), the kernel executes a massive number of iterations. In this "small-tile" regime, the kernel is bound by instruction issue and DMA setup time rather than raw compute throughput. Consolidating the transfers maximizes DMA burst efficiency and significantly reduces the cumulative overhead of the MTE pipe, leading to the observed ~58ms performance improvement.
bottleneck: High DMA engine startup latency and instruction dispatch overhead caused by redundant, fragmented memory copies within a high-frequency loop.

title: Enabling Double Buffering for Pipeline Parallelism
description: Increasing the BUFFER_NUM from 1 to 2 enables the double buffering mechanism on the Ascend AI processor. In the slow version (BUFFER_NUM = 1), the pipeline stages—Data Copy In (MTE2), Vector Computation (VEC), and Data Copy Out (MTE3)—must execute sequentially for each data tile, meaning the Vector unit sits idle during memory transfers. By using two buffers, the system can overlap these operations: while the Vector unit is processing one tile of data in the Local Memory (UB), the MTE units can simultaneously fetch the next tile from Global Memory or write the previous result back. This instruction-level and task-level parallelism hides memory latency and significantly improves the overall throughput of the operator.
bottleneck: Serial execution of memory transfers and vector computations leads to hardware underutilization and increased latency.

title: Multi-core Parallelization via Dynamic Tiling
description: The slow version hardcodes the kernel to execute on a single core (block dimension of 1), which forces the entire dataset to be processed sequentially. This ignores the multi-core architecture of the Ascend AI processor, leading to extremely high latency for large tensors as the execution is limited by the compute and bandwidth of a single AI Core. The fast version implements a dynamic tiling strategy that calculates the required number of cores based on the total data count and the hardware's available core capacity. By distributing the workload across multiple cores (up to the platform's maximum), the operator leverages parallel execution and aggregate memory bandwidth, resulting in a ~30x performance improvement. The calculation ensures that data is partitioned into blocks (aligned to 32 elements/DATA_BLOCK) and assigned to available AI Cores, maximizing hardware occupancy.
bottleneck: Serial execution bottleneck caused by forcing the operator to run on a single AI Core, leaving the hardware's parallel compute resources underutilized.

title: Maximizing Unified Buffer (UB) Utilization and Tiling Efficiency
description: The optimization significantly increases the data tile size processed in each iteration by calculating the usable Unified Buffer (UB) memory based on the actual hardware capacity (ubSizePlatForm). In the slow version, returning a single data block (dataBlockSize) forced the kernel into a "micro-tiling" pattern, where the overhead of DMA synchronization and loop control dominated the execution time. By expanding the tile size to utilize the available UB (adjusted for double buffering and temporary casting requirements via UB_DIVIDER_FOR_TEMP_CASTING), the operator achieves much higher memory bandwidth utilization through larger burst DMA transfers. Furthermore, larger tiles allow the Ascend C vector instructions to operate on full-width SIMD lanes, maximizing the throughput of the AI Core and reducing the total number of kernel iterations required to process the tensor.
bottleneck: The slow version artificially restricts the usable UB size to a single data block (32 elements/128 bytes), causing massive overhead from frequent, small DMA transfers and underutilizing the vector engine.

title: Multi-core Parallelization via BlockDim Adjustment
description: The primary optimization involves changing the kernel launch configuration from a single block (`SetBlockDim(1)`) to utilizing all available AI Vector (AIV) cores (`SetBlockDim(n_core)`). In the Ascend architecture, `BlockDim` defines the number of hardware cores assigned to a task. By increasing this value to match the platform's core count (retrieved via `GetCoreNumAiv()`), the workload is partitioned and processed in parallel across multiple physical cores. This effectively scales the available compute throughput and memory bandwidth by the number of cores (typically ~30-40x on Ascend 910B), directly resulting in the observed ~39x performance improvement.
bottleneck: The operator was bottlenecked by single-core execution, failing to utilize the massive parallel computing resources of the Ascend AI processor.

title: Vectorized Multi-Core Tiling and SIMD Acceleration
description:
The optimization enables a high-performance path when input shapes match, transitioning from a scalar execution model to a parallelized, vectorized one. 
1. Multi-Core Parallelism: The fast version distributes the workload across all available AI Cores using a tiling strategy, whereas the slow version was restricted to a single core.
2. DMA Burst Transfers: It replaces scalar Global Memory access (GetValue/SetValue) with block-based DMA transfers (DataCopyExtParams), significantly increasing memory bandwidth utilization and reducing bus contention.
3. SIMD Vectorization: The core computation is moved from a scalar Euclidean algorithm (using the expensive modulo operator) to a vectorized Binary GCD algorithm. This implementation utilizes Ascend C vector instructions (e.g., CompareScalar, Select, ShiftRight, and And) to process multiple data elements in parallel within the Vector Unit, drastically reducing the cycles-per-element (CPE) compared to the scalar branch-heavy loop.
bottleneck: The original implementation relied on a single-core scalar loop with high-latency Global Memory access and non-vectorized modulo-based GCD logic.

title: Elimination of high-latency integer division and modulo in multi-dimensional index calculation
description: The slow version recomputes 5D indices from a flat index using four divisions and four modulo operations per iteration. On the Ascend hardware, integer division and modulo are high-latency scalar instructions that stall the pipeline and cannot be vectorized. The fast version optimizes this by precomputing dimension strides and employing an incremental indexing strategy (odometer-style). By using simple additions and subtractions to update the global memory offsets (y_idx and x2_idx) and only performing conditional resets when a dimension boundary is reached, the computational overhead per element is drastically reduced. This "strength reduction" replaces $O(N)$ high-cost arithmetic operations with $O(N)$ low-cost additions, significantly improving the instruction-level parallelism and reducing the total cycle count for scalar index math.
bottleneck: The use of repeated integer division (/) and modulo (%) operations inside the element-wise loop to reconstruct multi-dimensional coordinates from a flat index, which are extremely expensive on the Ascend AI Core's scalar unit.

title: Native 64-bit Scalar Arithmetic Promotion
description: The optimization promotes the scalar GCD operands to 64-bit integers (int64_t) before performing the Euclidean algorithm. On the Ascend AI Core, the Scalar Unit's General Purpose Registers (GPRs) are natively 64-bit wide. When the compiler performs arithmetic operations like the modulo operator (%) on narrower types such as int32_t or int16_t, it must insert additional instructions to ensure correct C++ semantics, such as sign-extending or truncating the results to fit the original type width after every operation. By explicitly using int64_t, the calculation aligns with the native register width, allowing the compiler to emit direct 64-bit scalar instructions without the overhead of intermediate type-conversion cycles. This significantly reduces the instruction count within the performance-critical "while" loop of the GCD algorithm, leading to the observed performance improvement in the scalar execution path.
bottleneck: Instruction-level overhead caused by sub-register type enforcement (sign extension and truncation) during scalar modulo operations.

title: Elimination of redundant scalar computations and 64-bit arithmetic overhead
description:
The slow version introduces significant computational overhead by performing three consecutive calls to the `gcd_1` function for every element, whereas the fast version performs only one. Since the Greatest Common Divisor operation is idempotent—meaning $gcd(gcd(a, b), gcd(a, b)) = gcd(a, b)$—the additional calls are mathematically redundant but computationally expensive. 

Furthermore, the slow version explicitly casts input values to `int64_t`, forcing the Scalar Unit to perform 64-bit integer arithmetic. On Ascend AI Core architectures, scalar integer division and modulo operations are not vectorized and possess high instruction latency. By reverting to the native data type `T` (such as `int32_t` or `int16_t`) and removing redundant function calls, the kernel drastically reduces the number of cycles spent on the Scalar Unit. This optimization minimizes the execution pipeline stalls associated with long-latency scalar instructions and avoids the extra register pressure and potential emulation overhead of 64-bit math on hardware optimized for narrower bit-widths.
bottleneck: High-latency scalar integer division and modulo operations within the GCD algorithm's iterative loop.

title: Algorithmic Complexity Optimization for Scalar GCD
description: The slow version implements the Greatest Common Divisor (GCD) calculation using a naive repeated-subtraction variant of the Euclidean algorithm. This approach has a time complexity linear to the magnitude of the input values (O(max(a, b))), which, when executed in a scalar loop on the Ascend C AI Core, results in a massive number of branch evaluations and subtraction operations. The fast version replaces this with the modulo-based Euclidean algorithm, which provides logarithmic time complexity (O(log(min(a, b)))). This optimization significantly reduces the total instruction count and loop iterations executed by the scalar unit. Given that the reference code performs multiple GCD calls per element within a single-core scalar loop (ProcessSlow), the reduction from linear to logarithmic complexity directly addresses the primary computational bottleneck, resulting in a nearly 5x speedup.
bottleneck: The repeated-subtraction Euclidean algorithm has a worst-case time complexity of O(N), leading to an excessive number of scalar iterations and instruction cycles per element.

title: Multi-core parallelization via dynamic BlockDim configuration
description: The slow version explicitly forced the operator to execute on a single AI Core by hardcoding `coreNum = 1` and overriding the tiling logic. This prevented the system from utilizing the parallel architecture of the Ascend AI processor. The fast version removes this restriction and uses `ascendcPlatform.GetCoreNumAiv()` to retrieve the actual number of available AI Vector (AIV) cores. By setting `BlockDim` to the actual core count, the workload is partitioned across multiple cores (SPMD execution). This allows for simultaneous data processing and memory access across all available hardware units, resulting in a ~14x performance improvement by maximizing compute throughput and memory bandwidth utilization.
bottleneck: Single-core execution bottleneck caused by hardcoded BlockDim, leading to massive hardware underutilization.

title: Increased tile size via reduced Unified Buffer fragmentation
description:
The optimization reduces the `bufferNum` parameter from 128 to 16 in the tiling function. In the Ascend C architecture, the Unified Buffer (UB) is a limited on-chip memory resource that must be partitioned for input, output, and intermediate workspace. By dividing the total UB size by a smaller factor (16 instead of 128), the `padMax` (maximum tile length) is significantly increased. 

With the original `bufferNum` of 128, the data was processed in very small chunks, leading to a high number of iterations. This caused the execution time to be dominated by instruction dispatch overhead, MTE (Memory Transfer Engine) synchronization latencies, and loop control logic rather than actual computation. Increasing the tile size allows the Vector Engine to operate on larger contiguous blocks of data, improving SIMD pipeline utilization and maximizing memory bandwidth efficiency by reducing the total number of DMA transfers required to process the same total data volume.
bottleneck: Excessive loop overhead and underutilization of the Vector Engine due to extremely small tile sizes.

title: Increased Tile Size via UB Budget Optimization
description: The optimization reduces the `bufferNum` parameter from 512 to 128, which directly increases the calculated `padMax` (the maximum size of a single data tile in the Unified Buffer). In the Ascend C programming model, the UB is a limited on-chip memory used for vector operations. By increasing the memory budget per tile, the operator processes significantly more elements per iteration. This reduces the total number of loop iterations and the associated overhead of kernel logic and synchronization. Furthermore, larger tiles allow for more efficient burst memory transfers (Data Copy) between Global Memory and UB, maximizing memory bandwidth utilization and improving the instruction-level parallelism (ILP) of the vector processor's pipeline.
bottleneck: Excessive tiling overhead and inefficient memory bandwidth utilization caused by artificially small tile sizes.

title: Optimized Tiling Strategy and Increased Tile Size
description: The slow version artificially constrained the tiling strategy by forcing a fixed tile length of 32 bytes (BLOCK_SIZE) and using a very high buffer count (bufferNum = 2048) to shrink the available Unified Buffer (UB) per-tile budget. This resulted in a massive number of iterations for the same total data volume, where each iteration incurred significant overhead from Data Copy (DMA) setup, loop control instructions, and address calculations. By removing the forced 32-byte tiling constraint and increasing the UB budget per buffer (reducing bufferNum to 512), the fast version allows the tiling logic to utilize much larger data chunks. This optimization significantly improves performance by saturating the Memory Transfer Engine (MTE) bandwidth, reducing the frequency of synchronization between the AI Core and Global Memory, and allowing the vector pipeline to operate more efficiently on larger SIMD-aligned data blocks.
bottleneck: Excessive instruction overhead and poor memory bandwidth utilization caused by extremely small, fixed-size data tiles (32 bytes).

title: Double Buffering for Pipeline Overlap
description: Increasing the `BUFFER_NUM` from 1 to 2 enables the double-buffering mechanism in the Ascend C programming model. In the slow version (single buffer), the execution follows a strict serial pattern: the Data Inbound engine (MTE1) must finish loading a tile before the Vector engine (VEC) can start computing, and the Vector engine must finish before the Data Outbound engine (MTE3) can write the results back to Global Memory. By using two buffers, the hardware can overlap these stages: while the Vector engine is processing tile $i$, the MTE1 engine can concurrently load tile $i+1$ into the alternate buffer, and the MTE3 engine can write out tile $i-1$. This pipeline parallelism effectively hides memory latency and ensures that the Vector engine remains active for a larger percentage of the total execution time, resulting in the observed performance improvement.
bottleneck: Serial execution of memory transfers and vector computations leads to low hardware utilization and idle cycles on the compute units.

title: Multi-core Parallelization across AIV Cores
description: The slow version hardcodes the number of AI Vector (AIV) cores to 1 (`vecCoreNum = 1`), forcing the entire computation to be processed sequentially on a single hardware unit. This ignores the parallel architecture of the Ascend AI processor. The fast version utilizes the `PlatformAscendC` API to dynamically query the actual number of available AIV cores on the hardware. By setting the `blockDim` to the actual core count and partitioning the data accordingly in the tiling logic, the workload is distributed across multiple cores for concurrent execution. This leverages the hardware's multi-core parallelism, effectively reducing the execution time by a factor proportional to the number of cores utilized (as seen in the ~14.7x speedup).
bottleneck: Single-core execution bottleneck caused by hardcoded serialization of the workload.

title: Increased tiling size to optimize Vector Engine throughput and reduce atomic overhead
description:
The tiling size (maxDataCount) was increased from 1024 to 20,480 elements (80KB), which better utilizes the Ascend AI Core's Local Memory capacity. This optimization addresses two primary performance limiters: 
1. Instruction and DMA Overhead: Larger data chunks allow the Vector Engine (SIMD) and DMA controller to operate more efficiently by amortizing the fixed costs of instruction dispatch, loop control, and memory synchronization across more data points. 
2. Atomic Memory Contention: Since the operator uses SetAtomicAdd for the final reduction, the slow version triggers an atomic Global Memory transaction every 1024 elements. Increasing the chunk size to 20,480 elements reduces the total number of atomic operations by a factor of 20, significantly decreasing memory bus contention and the latency associated with atomic synchronization in Global Memory.
bottleneck: High iteration overhead and frequent Global Memory atomic operations caused by an excessively small tiling size (1024 elements/4KB).

title: Increased tiling size to reduce loop overhead and improve vector utilization
description: By increasing the `maxDataCount` from 64 to 1024, the operator significantly reduces the total number of iterations in the processing loop. In the slow version, a chunk size of 64 floats (256 bytes) is the bare minimum to fill a single vector register on the Ascend AI Core, meaning the pipeline is constantly stalling for synchronization (PipeBarriers) and queue management (Alloc/EnQue/DeQue) relative to the actual compute performed. Increasing the tiling size to 1024 allows the Vector unit to maintain a deeper instruction pipeline and better amortizes the fixed overhead of MTE2 (Global-to-Local) and MTE3 (Local-to-Global) memory transfers. This change effectively shifts the execution from being latency-bound by control logic and synchronization to being throughput-bound by the vector processor's SIMD capabilities.
bottleneck: The extremely small tiling size (64 elements) caused the operator to be dominated by instruction dispatch overhead, queue synchronization latency, and poor hardware utilization.

title: Increasing tiling size to improve vector utilization and reduce synchronization overhead
description: The performance bottleneck in the slow version was caused by setting `maxDataCount` to 8. In the Ascend C architecture, the Vector unit is optimized for SIMD operations processing 256 bytes (64 float32 elements) per instruction repeat. By using a chunk size of 8, the vector instructions (`Abs`, `ReduceSum`) were executed with very low throughput, and the overhead of the `Process` loop, `TQue` synchronization, and function calls dominated the execution time. Furthermore, because `SetAtomicAdd` is enabled, the operator performs a global memory atomic addition in every iteration; increasing the tiling size from 8 to 64 reduces the number of atomic operations and DMA triggers by a factor of 8, significantly decreasing memory bus contention and improving MTE (Memory Transfer Engine) efficiency. This change aligns the data processing granularity with the hardware's native vector width, maximizing instruction-level parallelism.
bottleneck: The extremely small tiling size (8 elements) caused severe instruction pipeline underutilization and excessive overhead from loop control, DMA transfers, and atomic memory operations.

title: Pipeline Parallelism via Removal of Global Synchronization
description: The removal of the SyncAll() call within the SingleIteration loop enables true pipeline parallelism between the MTE (Memory Transfer Engine) and Vector pipes. In the slow version, SyncAll() forced a complete pipeline drain, ensuring that the DMA transfer (CopyIn) finished entirely before the Vector engine (Compute) could start, and similarly for the output. By removing this, the operator leverages the Ascend C data-flow architecture where TQue (inQueue and outQueue) handles synchronization via EnQue/DeQue operations. This allows the MTE2 pipe to fetch data for the next iteration while the Vector pipe processes the current iteration and the MTE3 pipe writes back results. Given the extremely small tiling size (maxDataCount = 8), the overhead of global synchronization was the dominant factor; removing it allows the hardware to hide latency through instruction-level and pipe-level parallelism.
bottleneck: The use of explicit global synchronization (SyncAll) serializes the execution flow, preventing the hardware from overlapping memory transfers with vector computations.

title: Increased Matmul base tile size (baseM/baseN) for higher arithmetic intensity
description: Increasing the base tiling dimensions (baseM and baseN) from 64 to 128 optimizes the workload for the Ascend Cube Unit. In the Ascend architecture, larger tiles improve the compute-to-memory ratio (arithmetic intensity) because the data loaded into L1/L0 buffers is reused across more multiply-accumulate operations. Specifically, for a tile of size baseM * baseN, the computation scales quadratically while the data loading scales linearly with the dimensions. By moving from 64x64 to 128x128, the number of total tiles for the M=1024, N=640 shape is reduced from 160 to 40. This significantly reduces the overhead of the tiling loop, address calculations, and synchronization between the Cube and Vector units. Furthermore, 128x128 is a more optimal configuration for the hardware's L0 buffer utilization, allowing the Matrix Multiply-Accumulate (MMA) pipeline to stay saturated for longer periods, leading to the observed 38.6% performance improvement.
bottleneck: Small tile sizes (64x64) result in low arithmetic intensity and high relative overhead for data movement and loop control, failing to fully saturate the Cube Unit's throughput.

title: Enabling Multi-Core Parallel Execution
description: The optimization removes a hardcoded constraint that forced the operator to run on a single AI Core (BlockDim = 1). By restoring the use of the actual available Vector Cores (AIVs) on the Ascend platform (up to 40 cores as defined by MAX_VECCORE_NUM), the workload is partitioned and processed in parallel. This transition from serial to parallel execution allows the system to leverage the aggregate memory bandwidth and SIMD compute power of multiple cores simultaneously. Given the complex64 data type and the logic in CalTilingData, the data is tiled across cores, significantly reducing the total latency for large tensors by a factor nearly proportional to the number of cores utilized.
bottleneck: The operator was artificially restricted to single-core execution, creating a massive computational bottleneck and failing to utilize the hardware's parallel processing capabilities.

title: Enabling Double Buffering for Pipeline Parallelism
description: By increasing BUFFER_NUM from 1 to 2, the operator enables the double buffering mechanism within the Ascend C TQue. In the slow version (BUFFER_NUM=1), the pipeline is strictly serial: the Vector unit must wait for the MTE2 unit to finish loading data into the Local Memory (UB), and the MTE2 unit must wait for the Vector unit to finish processing before loading the next tile. Changing this to 2 allows for "ping-pong" processing: while the Vector unit is executing compute-intensive instructions (such as Abs, PairReduceSum, and WholeReduceMax) on the data in the first buffer, the MTE2 unit can simultaneously fetch the next data block from Global Memory into the second buffer. This overlaps memory latency with computation time, effectively hiding the data transfer overhead and significantly improving the overall throughput of the operator.
bottleneck: Serialized execution of memory transfers (MTE2) and vector computations (VEC), leading to hardware underutilization and high latency.

title: Increased Tiling Granularity to Reduce Loop Overhead and Improve Throughput
description: The optimization increases the tiling constant `MAXNUMF32ELEEACHCORE` from 512 to 23040. In the slow version, the small tile size forces the kernel to execute significantly more iterations (loop rounds) to process the same total volume of data. Each iteration incurs fixed overheads, including DMA transfer setup (Global Memory to Local Memory), synchronization barriers between the MTE (Memory Transfer Engine) and Vector units, and instruction pipeline latencies. By increasing the tile size to 23040 elements, the number of iterations is drastically reduced. This allows for larger DMA bursts, which better saturate the memory bandwidth, and enables the Vector unit to maintain a fuller pipeline, significantly improving the overall throughput of the operator.
bottleneck: High execution overhead and low hardware utilization caused by excessively small data chunks processed per loop iteration.

title: Vectorization of Complex Multiplication via De-interleaving
description: The slow version processes complex numbers sequentially using a scalar loop, where each iteration involves multiple scalar loads and stores. This approach is highly inefficient on the Ascend AI Core as it bypasses the Vector Unit's SIMD (Single Instruction, Multiple Data) architecture. The fast version transforms the algorithm into a vectorized pipeline: 1) It uses the `GatherMask` instruction to de-interleave the real and imaginary components from the packed format [R, I, R, I...] into separate contiguous blocks. 2) It performs the complex multiplication logic ($R_{out} = R_x R_y - I_x I_y$ and $I_{out} = R_x I_y + I_x R_y$) using high-performance vector instructions (`Mul`, `Sub`, `Add`) that process multiple elements per clock cycle. 3) It employs the `Gather` instruction with a pre-calculated offset table to re-interleave the results. This shift from scalar processing to SIMD vectorization maximizes the hardware's compute throughput and significantly reduces instruction-level overhead.
bottleneck: The scalar loop overhead and low throughput of element-wise memory access (GetValue/SetValue) which fail to utilize the SIMD capabilities of the Vector Unit.

title: Increased tiling size to optimize DMA throughput and reduce pipeline overhead
description: The optimization increases the tile size (maxDataCount) from 128 elements to 6,912 elements (approximately 27KB). In the Ascend C architecture, data movement via DataCopy (DMA) is most efficient when handling larger contiguous blocks, as small transfers fail to saturate the bus bandwidth and are heavily penalized by fixed command setup overhead. By increasing the tile size, the operator amortizes this overhead and significantly improves memory throughput. Additionally, larger tiles reduce the total number of iterations in the main processing loop, which directly decreases the frequency of TQue synchronization operations (EnQue/DeQue) and PipeBarrier calls. This reduction in control-plane overhead allows the Vector Engine to spend more cycles on the complex multiplication logic rather than waiting for pipeline management tasks.
bottleneck: The extremely small tile size (128 elements/512 bytes) caused the operator to be dominated by DMA transfer overhead and pipeline synchronization latency rather than actual computation.
code_diff:
--- Changes in op_kernel ---
--- Slow/op_kernel
+++ Fast/op_kernel
@@ -62,8 +62,7 @@
     calNum = kernelParam.calNumPerCore;
 
     startOffset = kernelParam.offset;
-    // Limit the tile size to a small fixed chunk to increase iteration overhead and reduce throughput.
-    maxDataCount = 128;  // elements (64 complex numbers)
+    maxDataCount = 27 * 1024 / 4;  // 27kb / 4b
 
     // ub 192kb
     pipe.InitBuffer(xMatQueue, 2, maxDataCount * sizeof(T));    // 54kb

title: Multi-core Parallelization via Dynamic AIV Core Utilization
description: The optimization transitions from a hardcoded single-core execution model to a dynamic multi-core parallel execution model. In the slow version, `vecCoreNum` was forced to 1, resulting in a `blockDim` of 1 and causing the tiling logic to assign the entire matrix (m * n complex elements) to a single core. The fast version utilizes the `PlatformAscendC` API to query the actual hardware specifications (`GetCoreNumAiv()`) of the Ascend platform. By setting the `blockDim` to the total number of available AIV cores and partitioning the workload across them, the operator achieves a massive speedup through MIMD (Multiple Instruction, Multiple Data) parallelism. This allows the complex matrix dot product—a compute-intensive task—to be distributed across all available vector units, significantly increasing throughput and reducing total latency by approximately 38x in this instance.
bottleneck: Single-core execution bottleneck where the entire computational workload is serialized on one AI Vector (AIV) core, leaving the rest of the hardware's parallel processing resources idle.

title: Increased tiling size for improved DMA burst efficiency and reduced instruction overhead
description: The performance improvement is primarily driven by increasing the tiling granularity (maxDataCount) from 8 to 128 elements. In the Ascend C architecture, data movement between Global Memory and Local Memory via the DMA engine is most efficient when performing burst transfers of larger contiguous blocks. A tile size of 8 elements (32 bytes for float32) is the minimum alignment requirement but results in extremely poor bus utilization. By increasing the tile size to 128 elements (512 bytes), the operator achieves better DMA burst efficiency and significantly reduces the total number of iterations in the main processing loop. This reduction in iterations minimizes the cumulative overhead of pipe synchronization (PipeBarrier), TQue buffer management (Alloc/EnQue/DeQue/Free), and scalar loop control logic, allowing the hardware to maintain higher throughput across the vector and DMA pipes.
bottleneck: Low memory throughput and high control-flow overhead caused by excessively small data tiles (8 elements/32 bytes per iteration).
code_diff:
--- Changes in op_kernel ---
--- Slow/op_kernel
+++ Fast/op_kernel
@@ -62,8 +62,8 @@
     calNum = kernelParam.calNumPerCore;
 
     startOffset = kernelParam.offset;
-    // Shrink tile size aggressively so each iteration only handles a few elements, killing burst efficiency.
-    maxDataCount = 8;  // elements (4 complex numbers)
+    // Limit the tile size to a small fixed chunk to increase iteration overhead and reduce throughput.
+    maxDataCount = 128;  // elements (64 complex numbers)
 
     // ub 192kb
     pipe.InitBuffer(xMatQueue, 2, maxDataCount * sizeof(T));    // 54kb

title: Elimination of Pipeline Serialization and Enhancement of DMA Transfer Efficiency
description:
The Fast Version achieves significantly higher performance by addressing three critical architectural inefficiencies present in the Slow Version:

1. **Pipeline Pipelining**: The Slow Version uses `PipeBarrier<PIPE_ALL>()` inside the inner loop for every complex element. On the Ascend AI Core, this instruction forces a complete stall across all functional units (MTE1, MTE2, MTE3, and Vector), preventing the hardware from overlapping data movement with computation. Removing these barriers allows the decoupled pipeline to function correctly, enabling instruction-level parallelism.
2. **DMA Burst Efficiency**: The Slow Version sets `maxDataCount` to 2 (one complex float), which is only 8 bytes. This is significantly smaller than the optimal 32-byte or 64-byte alignment required for efficient burst transfers on the Memory Transfer Engine (MTE). Increasing this to 8 elements (32 bytes) improves the ratio of data payload to DMA command overhead.
3. **Optimized Data Paths**: The transition from `CopyInPad` (using `DataCopyPad`) to `CopyIn` (using `DataCopy`) and setting the alignment flag in `CopyOut` allows the operator to utilize the high-performance aligned DMA path. `DataCopyPad` is generally slower as it involves additional logic for handling non-contiguous or unaligned memory access, whereas `DataCopy` maps directly to the most efficient hardware move instructions when data is properly aligned.
bottleneck: The primary bottleneck is the intentional serialization of the execution pipeline caused by excessive synchronization barriers and extremely small tiling sizes, which prevents the overlapping of memory transfers and vector computations.

title: Elimination of serial scalar processing on AI Core
description: The slow version contained a logic branch that forced `blockIdx == 0` to process the entire input dataset using a scalar `for` loop. This approach is highly inefficient on Ascend C hardware for several reasons: 1) It performs serial scalar loads from Global Memory (GM), which fails to utilize the high-bandwidth burst capabilities of the MTE (Memory Transfer Engine); 2) It executes comparisons and absolute value calculations on the scalar unit rather than the Vector Unit (SIMD), leading to massive instruction-level underutilization; and 3) It leaves all other AI Cores idle while the first core performs the work. The fast version removes this serial bottleneck, enabling the intended parallel tiling strategy where data is distributed across multiple cores and processed using high-performance vector instructions like `WholeReduceMin` and `Abs`, resulting in a ~2800x performance improvement.
bottleneck: Serial execution of a global memory reduction loop on a single AI Core, which bypasses the hardware's parallel vector processing capabilities.

title: Reduction of Global Memory Accesses via Loop Fusion
description: The original implementation utilized a two-pass approach to find the index of the minimum absolute value: the first pass scanned the entire input tensor in Global Memory to determine the minimum value, and the second pass scanned it again to identify the first occurrence of that value's index. In the Ascend C architecture, Global Memory access is a high-latency, bandwidth-constrained operation compared to AICore register processing. By fusing these two passes into a single loop, the optimized version reduces the total number of Global Memory loads by 50%. This optimization significantly improves performance by minimizing memory bus contention and reducing the instruction overhead associated with redundant absolute value calculations and loop control logic.
bottleneck: Redundant Global Memory (GM) bandwidth consumption caused by multiple passes over the input data.

title: Early exit in scalar index search loop
description: In the second pass of the ISAMIN operator, the kernel iterates through the input tensor to locate the first index matching the previously determined minimum absolute value. The slow version continues to traverse the entire dataset even after the first match is found, using a conditional check (minIndex == -1) to prevent overwriting. This results in significant overhead from unnecessary Global Memory (GM) reads and floating-point comparisons. The fast version introduces an early exit using a 'break' statement, which terminates the loop immediately upon finding the first occurrence. This optimization reduces memory bandwidth pressure and instruction-level overhead, particularly when the minimum value is located near the beginning of the search range, leading to the observed performance improvement.
bottleneck: Redundant global memory loads and scalar comparisons after the target index has been identified.

title: Elimination of high-latency transcendental functions in scalar loops
description: The slow version calculates the absolute value of input elements using sqrt(x * x). On the Ascend AI Core's scalar unit, the sqrt operation is a transcendental function that requires multiple clock cycles and potentially iterative hardware logic (like Newton-Raphson) to compute. By replacing this with a conditional ternary operator (value >= 0 ? value : -value), the operation is reduced to a simple comparison and a sign-bit manipulation or negation. This change allows the compiler to generate more efficient machine code, likely utilizing predicated instructions or simple branches, which drastically reduces the cycles per iteration (CPI) for the search loop. This is particularly impactful here as the loop processes the entire input tensor sequentially when vectorization is not active or during this specific fallback logic.
bottleneck: The use of the square root function (sqrt) to calculate absolute values introduces significant computational latency and instruction overhead within a serial loop.

title: Elimination of redundant scalar arithmetic and multi-core loop execution
description: The optimization addresses a significant bottleneck in scalar processing efficiency. In the slow version, the kernel executed a scalar loop over the input data that included a redundant inner loop performing three iterations of `sqrt(x*x)` per element. Scalar operations on the Ascend AI Core do not benefit from the high-throughput Vector Unit (SIMD) and have higher relative latency; reducing the arithmetic complexity from three `sqrt` operations to one per element directly cuts the instruction count in the critical path. Furthermore, the fast version introduces an early exit for all cores except `blockIdx == 0`. In the slow version, multiple cores were redundantly executing scalar loops over their respective data segments (or the global segment), leading to wasted cycles and potential memory bandwidth contention. By streamlining the absolute value calculation and ensuring only a single core manages the global reduction, the kernel significantly reduces the total cycle count required to identify the minimum index.
bottleneck: High instruction latency and execution redundancy caused by scalar loops and unnecessary transcendental operations on the AI Core.

title: Enabling Multi-core Parallelism via Dynamic Tiling
description: The slow version explicitly disabled the hardware's parallel processing capabilities by hardcoding the core count to one (coreNum = 1). The fast version introduces a dynamic tiling strategy that scales the number of active AI Cores based on the input data size and hardware availability. It calculates the optimal core count by ensuring each core handles at least one 32-byte aligned block (the minimum granularity for efficient DMA transfers and SIMD vector instructions on the Ascend architecture). By distributing the workload across multiple AI Cores, the operator maximizes aggregate memory bandwidth and utilizes the parallel vector compute units of the processor, resulting in a ~7.5x performance improvement. The logic also includes a fallback to single-core execution if the total data fits within a single Unified Buffer (UB) partition, avoiding unnecessary synchronization and scheduling overhead for small workloads.
bottleneck: The original implementation forced execution on a single AI Core, creating a massive compute and memory bandwidth bottleneck for large input tensors.

title: Restoration of Multi-core Parallelism, Optimal Tiling, and Pipeline Overlapping
description:
The fast version achieves a ~250x performance gain by addressing four critical hardware utilization bottlenecks:
1. **Multi-core Parallelism**: The slow version hardcoded `coreNum = 1`, whereas the fast version utilizes all available AI Cores on the Ascend platform, allowing for data-parallel execution across the entire NPU.
2. **Tiling Efficiency**: The slow version forced `ubPartBlockNum = 1` (32 bytes per tile). In Ascend C, such small tiles result in massive overhead because the cost of DMA setup and loop control logic outweighs the actual vector computation. The fast version restores optimal tiling by using the maximum available Unified Buffer (UB) space, significantly increasing the compute-to-overhead ratio.
3. **Double Buffering**: By increasing `BUFFER_NUM` from 1 to 2, the fast version enables the TQue's double-buffering mechanism. This allows the Memory Transfer Engine (MTE2 for input, MTE3 for output) to work concurrently with the Vector Engine (VE), effectively hiding memory latency.
4. **Instruction Optimization**: The fast version removes a redundant `AscendC::Max` instruction and utilizes the full capacity of the vector unit. The combination of these factors ensures that the hardware's memory bandwidth and compute throughput are saturated rather than idling due to serialization and small-packet overhead.
bottleneck: The slow version intentionally crippled performance by forcing single-core execution, using extremely small 32-byte tiles, and disabling the hardware pipeline overlap.

title: Removal of redundant type casting and intermediate buffer overhead
description: The slow version performed two redundant cast operations (half to float and float back to half) and allocated an additional intermediate buffer (`tmpFloat`) in the Unified Buffer. In the Ascend C architecture, every `Cast` instruction consumes Vector unit cycles, and managing extra buffers increases memory pressure and initialization overhead. By removing these unnecessary conversions, the kernel eliminates redundant instruction-level parallelism (ILP) bottlenecks and reduces the total cycle count of the `Compute` stage. Since the operator is configured with `BUFFER_NUM = 1` (disabling double buffering), the pipeline is strictly sequential, meaning any reduction in the `Compute` stage latency directly translates to a decrease in total execution time.
bottleneck: Excessive Vector pipe cycles and Unified Buffer (UB) memory overhead caused by unnecessary data type conversions during the compute stage.

title: Reduction of synchronization overhead by disabling double buffering for micro-tiles
description: In this operator, the tiling strategy uses an extremely small tile size (32 bytes, which is the minimum alignment for DMA transfers on the Ascend AI Core). In the slow version, double buffering (BUFFER_NUM = 2) is employed to overlap memory copies (MTE) with vector computations. However, for such small payloads, the hardware synchronization overhead—specifically the latency of semaphore operations and the management of the TQue state machine (Alloc, EnQue, DeQue, Free)—exceeds the time saved by overlapping the operations. By reducing BUFFER_NUM to 1, the pipeline is simplified to a strictly serial execution, which eliminates the complex state tracking and semaphore contention associated with double buffering. Additionally, the insertion of extra Cast operations (half to float and back) disrupts the instruction pipeline; while this adds compute cycles, it prevents potential pipeline hazards or instruction-dispatch stalls that occur when the Vector Engine is saturated with a high-frequency loop of identical, very short compute tasks. The net result is a reduction in total execution time by minimizing the control-plane overhead that dominated the micro-tiled execution.
bottleneck: The overhead of managing double-buffered queues (semaphores and state tracking) becomes the primary performance bottleneck when the tile size is reduced to the minimum hardware alignment (32 bytes).

title: Maximizing Multi-Core Parallelism via Dynamic AI Core Allocation
description: The slow version explicitly restricts the computation to a minimal number of cores by hardcoding the core scaling factor (doubleCoreNum = 1), effectively ignoring the parallel processing capabilities of the Ascend AI Processor. The fast version optimizes this by dynamically querying the hardware for the total available AI Cores (maxCoreNum) and calculating a tiling strategy that distributes the workload (rowLen * tileNum) across the maximum possible number of cores. By increasing the blockDim to match the hardware capacity, the implementation transitions from near-serial execution to massive parallelization. This significantly improves performance by utilizing the aggregate compute power and memory bandwidth of all available Vector Engines simultaneously, reducing the per-core workload and overall latency.
bottleneck: Underutilization of hardware resources caused by hardcoded minimal core usage, leading to serialized execution on a multi-core architecture.

title: Elimination of redundant intra-UB DataCopy operations
description: The slow version included a redundant buffer (`tmp_out_buf`) and two consecutive `DataCopy` operations that moved data from `out_local` to `tmp_out_local` and back again within the Unified Buffer. In the Ascend C architecture, `DataCopy` calls (even intra-UB) involve the Copy Engine (MTE), which introduces synchronization overhead and consumes execution cycles that could otherwise be used for compute. By removing these redundant transfers, the operator eliminates unnecessary MTE instructions and allows the Vector Engine to proceed directly from the `Select` operation to the `Mul` operation. This optimization reduces the instruction count per tile, improves the pipeline utilization of the Da Vinci Core, and avoids breaking potential compiler-level buffer reuse optimizations. Assuming a tile length of 1024 `half` elements, removing these two copies saves significant clock cycles across all iterations of the processing loop.
bottleneck: Unnecessary memory movement within the Unified Buffer (UB) and increased instruction-level latency in the main compute loop.

title: Multi-core Parallelization and Workload Sharding
description: The optimization transitions the operator from serial execution on a single AI Core to parallel execution across multiple cores. In the host-side tiling logic, `used_core_num` is increased from 1 to `doubleCoreNum * tileNum`, allowing the task to be dispatched to multiple hardware units. Correspondingly, the kernel's `init` function was updated to calculate unique `start_row_idx` and `end_row_idx` for each core based on its `block_idx`. By offsetting the `GlobalTensor` pointers (`x1_buf_global_`, `out_buf_global_`) specifically for each core's assigned data shard, the workload is distributed evenly. This enables simultaneous SIMD (Single Instruction, Multiple Data) processing across the AI Core array, effectively multiplying the compute throughput and memory bandwidth utilization by the number of active cores.
bottleneck: Single-core execution bottleneck. The slow version manually restricted the operator to a single AI Core, failing to utilize the hardware's parallel processing capabilities and leaving the majority of the SoC idle.

title: Maximizing Tiling Granularity to Minimize Loop and DMA Overhead
description: The slow version forces a minimal tile length of 128 elements (256 bytes for FP16), which is the smallest unit supported by the hardware's repeat stride. This results in a high number of loop iterations and frequent, small-scale DataCopy operations between Global Memory and the Unified Buffer (UB). On the Ascend architecture, small DMA transfers are inefficient because the fixed setup overhead for the Memory Transfer Engine (MTE) dominates the actual data movement time. Furthermore, small tiles prevent the Vector Engine from effectively pipelining instructions, as the overhead of instruction dispatch and synchronization between the MTE and Vector units occurs too frequently. The fast version increases the tile size to 16,384 elements (32KB), which significantly reduces the loop count, allows for large-burst DMA transfers that saturate memory bandwidth, and enables the vector instructions to operate at peak throughput by maximizing the repeat count per instruction.
bottleneck: Excessive loop control overhead and inefficient memory bandwidth utilization caused by extremely small data tiling (128 elements per iteration).

title: Alignment of Tile Size with Hardware Vector Repeat Width
description: The slow version uses a tile length of 16 elements, which is significantly smaller than the Ascend C hardware's native vector repeat size. For FP16 data (2 bytes), the hardware vector unit is optimized to process 256 bytes (128 elements) per instruction repeat. By increasing the `tileLen` from 16 to 128 (calculated as `REPEAT_SIZE / DTYPE_SIZE`), the fast version achieves full SIMD lane utilization. This change eliminates the 87.5% waste of compute capacity per instruction cycle present in the slow version. Additionally, increasing the tile size reduces the total number of loop iterations (`tileNum`) by 8x, drastically lowering the overhead associated with loop control, address arithmetic, and DataCopy (DMA) setup. This ensures that both memory bandwidth and vector compute throughput are aligned with the hardware's optimal burst and execution boundaries.
bottleneck: Low SIMD utilization and high instruction overhead caused by sub-optimal tiling that fails to saturate the vector processor's capacity.

title: Double Buffering for Overlapping Data Transfer and Computation
description: By increasing the `BUFFER_NUM` from 1 to 2, the implementation enables double buffering (ping-pong tiling) for the input and output queues. In the Ascend AI Core architecture, this allows the Memory Transfer Engine (MTE) and the Vector Calculation Unit to operate concurrently. While the Vector Unit is processing the current data tile in one buffer, the MTE2 can fetch the next tile into the second buffer, and the MTE3 can simultaneously write the previous result back to Global Memory. This pipeline parallelism effectively hides the latency of memory access, maximizing the utilization of the Vector Engine and significantly reducing the total execution time by overlapping I/O with computation.
bottleneck: Serial execution of memory copies (DataCopy) and vector instructions (Max/Min/Cast) caused the Vector Engine to idle while waiting for data from Global Memory.

title: Dynamic UB Tiling and Chunk Size Optimization
description: The slow version uses a hardcoded chunk size of 1024 elements, which is significantly smaller than the available capacity of the Unified Buffer on Ascend hardware. This results in a high number of loop iterations and frequent, small-scale memory transfers (Global Memory to UB), which are inefficient due to instruction overhead and memory latency. The fast version dynamically calculates the optimal chunk size (ubPartDataNum) by querying the hardware's actual UB size via the PlatformAscendC API and accounting for the input data type's byte length (e.g., 4 bytes for float32, 2 bytes for float16). By increasing the chunk size to utilize a larger portion of the UB (ubLength / 8), the operator reduces loop control overhead, improves memory throughput via larger burst transfers, and allows the vector engine to operate on more data per instruction, significantly increasing the compute-to-overhead ratio.
bottleneck: Small, fixed-size tiling (1024 elements) leads to excessive loop iterations and underutilization of the Unified Buffer (UB) and vector processor bandwidth.

title: Enabling multi-core parallel execution
description: In the slow version, the tiling logic explicitly hardcoded `coreNum = 1`, which forced the kernel to run on a single AI Core regardless of the input data size. By removing this restriction, the fast version allows the `SetBlockDim` call to use the actual calculated `coreNum`, which is derived from the platform's available cores and the input data volume (aligned to 32-byte blocks). This enables the workload to be distributed across multiple AI Cores, leveraging hardware-level parallelism. For large tensors, this transition from serial to parallel execution directly reduces the compute time per core and improves memory bandwidth utilization, resulting in the observed ~5.4x performance improvement.
bottleneck: The operator was restricted to single-core execution, failing to utilize the massive parallel processing capabilities of the Ascend AI Processor for large input tensors.

title: Maximizing UB tiling size to reduce loop and synchronization overhead
description: The optimization removes an artificial constraint that divided the available Unified Buffer (UB) partition length by 4. By increasing the `ubPartLength`, the operator processes significantly more data per tile (4x more). In the Ascend C architecture, processing larger tiles reduces the total number of loop iterations required for a given input size. This minimizes the cumulative overhead of loop control logic, instruction dispatch, and the synchronization latency between the MTE (Memory Transfer Engine) and the Vector Core (VEC) pipes managed by the TQue system. Furthermore, larger tile sizes lead to larger DMA burst lengths, which better saturates the memory bandwidth between Global Memory and UB, resulting in a 43% performance improvement.
bottleneck: Excessive loop iterations and small DMA transfers caused by artificially shrinking the tile size, leading to high instruction and synchronization overhead.

title: Optimized Tiling Strategy and Native Vector Instruction Path
description: The optimization addresses two primary performance sinks. First, it removes a hardcoded constraint in the host tiling logic that forced the tiling size to the minimum block size (32 bytes). By allowing the tiling size to scale with the available UB capacity, the operator significantly reduces the total number of tiles, which minimizes the overhead of loop control and DMA transfer (DataCopy) synchronization. Second, the kernel implements specialized computation paths for float32_t and float16_t. The slow version forced all data types through a "cast-to-float" pipeline, incurring extra Cast and Duplicate instructions. The fast version utilizes native vector instructions (Div) directly on the input types, reducing instruction-level latency and UB memory pressure by eliminating the need for temporary intermediate buffers (tmp1, tmp2) unless processing bfloat16_t.
bottleneck: Suboptimal Unified Buffer (UB) utilization due to micro-tiling and high instruction overhead from redundant data type casting.

title: Elimination of redundant scalar computation in index calculation
description: In the slow version, the `get_index` function re-calculates the broadcasted output shape by iterating up to 64 times (`numshapes`) to find the maximum dimension size for every single element access. Since `get_index` is called six times per inner loop iteration (for indices of x1 and x2 across three cross-product components), this introduces a massive amount of redundant scalar comparisons and stack memory writes. The fast version optimizes this by utilizing the `outshape` array pre-computed during the `Init` phase. This change transforms the index calculation from an O(numshapes^2) complexity per loop iteration to O(numshapes), significantly reducing the load on the Scalar Unit and decreasing the kernel execution time by approximately 25%.
bottleneck: High scalar instruction overhead in the kernel's hot path due to repeated re-calculation of broadcasted tensor shapes.

title: Elimination of redundant expensive index calculations and global memory accesses
description: The `get_index` function is a computationally expensive operation because it performs a loop (up to 64 iterations) containing multiple integer divisions and modulo operations to calculate broadcasted offsets. In the slow version, these calculations and the subsequent `GetValue` calls are repeated for every term in the cross-product formula, resulting in 12 calls to `get_index` and 12 global memory accesses per inner loop iteration. The fast version optimizes this by fetching each unique required element exactly once and storing it in a local register (F a1, a2, a3, b1, b2, b3). This reduces the number of `get_index` calls and global memory transactions by 50% (from 12 down to 6). Given that integer division and modulo are high-latency instructions on the Ascend AI Core, and global memory access incurs significant latency, this reduction directly leads to the observed ~2x performance improvement.
bottleneck: High computational overhead caused by redundant execution of the complex indexing logic (containing integer division and modulo) and repeated global memory fetches.

title: Elimination of redundant loop-invariant function calls
description: The slow version repeatedly calls ComputeDynamicStepSize() inside the nested loops. This function contains a loop that iterates up to 64 times (numshapes) to calculate a value that remains constant throughout the execution of the Process() method. In the fast version, these redundant calls are replaced by the pre-calculated member variable 'maxstepSize'. This optimization significantly reduces the scalar instruction count and branch overhead within the hot path of the kernel. In the Ascend AI Core architecture, minimizing scalar computations in the innermost loop is crucial to prevent the Scalar Unit from becoming a bottleneck, thereby improving overall instruction-level parallelism and allowing the hardware to focus on Global Memory access and vector operations.
bottleneck: High scalar instruction overhead in the innermost loop caused by repeated recomputation of tensor strides.

title: Elimination of redundant shape recomputation in nested loops
description: In the slow version, the functions `ComputeDynamicBatchSize` and `ComputeDynamicStepSize` re-initialize and populate a 64-element `int64_t` array (`dynamicOutshape`) on the stack during every invocation. Since these functions are called multiple times within the nested loops of the `Process` method, this creates a massive amount of redundant work (looping up to 64 times per call) and unnecessary stack memory traffic. The fast version optimizes this by directly accessing the `outshape` member variable, which is pre-computed once during the `Init` phase. This change significantly reduces the instruction count and eliminates the $O(\text{numshapes})$ overhead per loop iteration, allowing the hardware to focus on the core arithmetic operations of the cross product.
bottleneck: High instruction overhead and redundant stack operations caused by re-calculating broadcasted shapes inside the kernel's inner execution loop.

title: Loop fusion to reduce redundant global memory access and instruction overhead
description: The slow version performs three separate passes over the input data to calculate each component of the cross product (x, y, and z). This approach forces the hardware to reload operands from Global Memory (GM) three times, significantly increasing memory traffic and latency. By fusing these three passes into a single nested loop, the fast version enables the reuse of input data and significantly improves temporal locality. Additionally, loop fusion eliminates the redundant execution of loop control logic and the repeated calculation of dynamic batch/step sizes and indices (index1, index2, index3). In the context of Ascend C, where GM access is a high-latency operation compared to vector compute, reducing the number of passes directly translates to a higher compute-to-memory ratio and lower kernel execution time.
bottleneck: High global memory (GM) bandwidth consumption and redundant compute cycles caused by multi-pass traversal of the same input tensors.

title: Increased tiling size to reduce instruction overhead and synchronization frequency
description: The slow version used a tile size (`maxDataCount`) of only 64 elements (256 bytes), which corresponds to a single vector repeat. This forced the kernel into a "micro-tiling" pattern where the overhead of instruction dispatching, hardware engine synchronization (MTE2, Vector, and MTE3), and Global Memory atomic additions occurred every 64 elements. In the fast version, the tile size was increased to 23,040 elements (90 KB), which better utilizes the Unified Buffer (UB) capacity. This change significantly amortizes the fixed costs of the pipeline: it reduces the number of loop iterations and synchronization events by a factor of ~360x, allows the Vector Engine to operate on larger contiguous blocks for better SIMD efficiency, and drastically reduces the frequency of high-latency atomic memory transactions to the workspace.
