description: The optimization replaces the hardcoded single-core execution logic (max_core_num = 1) with dynamic hardware resource discovery using the PlatformAscendC API. By querying the actual number of available AI Vector (AIV) cores on the specific Ascend hardware (e.g., Ascend 910B), the tiling logic can now partition the input batch across multiple cores. This change allows the system to set a higher BlockDim, enabling the Task Scheduler to dispatch workloads in parallel. This transition from serial to parallel execution significantly improves throughput and reduces latency by utilizing the full SIMD processing power of the hardware's multi-core architecture, effectively scaling performance with the available compute units.
bottleneck: The operator was bottlenecked by single-core execution, where a hardcoded constraint forced the entire workload to be processed serially on one AI Core, leaving the rest of the hardware's compute resources idle.

title: Maximizing Unified Memory (UB) Utilization via Large-Block Tiling
description: In the slow version, the tiling parameter `elem_per_loop` was hardcoded to the minimum alignment size (`align_data`), forcing the kernel to process data in tiny chunks of 32 bytes (8 float32 elements). This approach fails to saturate the hardware's DMA bandwidth and incurs significant overhead for loop control and instruction scheduling. The fast version optimizes this by dynamically calculating the maximum allowable `elem_per_loop` based on the actual hardware Unified Memory (UB) capacity, accounting for double buffering and auxiliary space requirements. By maximizing the tiling size to fit the UB, the operator enables large-block DMA transfers and efficient SIMD vector processing, which drastically reduces the number of iterations and leverages the full throughput of the Ascend C architecture.
bottleneck: The operator was previously bottlenecked by extremely inefficient memory bandwidth utilization and high instruction overhead caused by a minimal tiling size (8 elements per loop).

title: Vectorized Index Calculation and Aligned Memory Tiling
description:
The optimization addresses two critical performance bottlenecks: 
1. Vectorization of Reductions: The slow version calculates the total output size and starting offsets using scalar 'for' loops and the 'GetValue()' method. In Ascend C, 'GetValue()' triggers a synchronization event between the Vector Unit (AIV) and the Scalar Unit, incurring significant latency when executed inside a loop. The fast version replaces these with the 'Sum' vector instruction, which performs high-speed parallel reduction on the AIV.
2. Memory Alignment and Throughput: The host tiling was modified to increase 'elem_per_loop' from 1 to 'align_data' (8 elements for FP32). Ascend hardware is optimized for 32-byte aligned memory transfers; processing a single element per loop (4 bytes) results in extremely low MTE (Memory Transfer Engine) utilization and high instruction overhead. By aligning the tiling size to the hardware's SIMD width, the kernel achieves significantly higher memory bandwidth and reduced loop control overhead.
bottleneck: High-latency scalar-to-vector synchronization and inefficient memory throughput caused by single-element tiling and manual scalar loops.

title: Multi-core Parallelization and Bulk Memory Transfer Optimization
description:
The performance improvement is primarily driven by two architectural optimizations:
1. Multi-core Parallelism: The slow version explicitly restricted execution to a single AI Core (useCoreNum = 1), leaving the majority of the NPU's compute resources idle. The fast version implements a tiling strategy that distributes the batch workload (dim0 * dim1) across all available AI Cores (totalCoreNum), enabling true hardware parallelism and significantly reducing the total execution time.
2. Bulk DMA Transfers (MTE3): On the kernel side, the slow version utilized a manual for-loop to perform DataCopy in segments. This approach incurs significant overhead due to repeated instruction issuance, address calculations, and loop control logic. The fast version replaces this with a single bulk DataCopy of the entire srcBatchStride. In the Ascend C programming model, a single large contiguous transfer allows the MTE3 (Memory Transfer Engine) to operate at peak burst bandwidth with minimal instruction-level overhead, drastically improving the efficiency of moving data from Local Memory (UB) to Global Memory (GM).
bottleneck: Serial execution on a single AI Core and high instruction overhead caused by fragmented memory transfers within the kernel loop.
